help me understand github and to get started on it asap

08.31

Here's a clear breakdown to get you up and running with GitHub fast.

The core idea: GitHub is a website where you store code online, track changes to it over time, and collaborate with others. It's built on top of a tool called Git, which runs on your computer and tracks changes locally. GitHub is the cloud layer on top.

Let me show you how the pieces fit together:

V

visualize

V

visualize show_widget

Now here's the actual workflow — what you'll type in your terminal day-to-day:

Step 1: Install Git Download from git-scm.com and install it. Then set your identity once:

bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"

Step 2: Create a GitHub account Go to github.com and sign up. It's free.

Step 3: Start a project You can either:

  • Create a new repo on GitHub first, then clone it to your computer: git clone https://github.com/yourname/yourrepo.git
  • Or start locally: git init inside a folder, then link it to GitHub later

Step 4: The daily loop Once you're set up, you'll repeat these 4 commands constantly:

bash
git add .           # stage all changed files
git commit -m "describe what you changed"
git push            # send to GitHub
git pull            # get others' changes (or your own from another machine)

Key concepts to know:

  • A repository (repo) is just a project folder with Git tracking enabled
  • A commit is a snapshot in time — like a save point you can go back to
  • A branch lets you work on something new without breaking the main code — git checkout -b my-new-feature
  • A pull request (PR) is how you propose your changes to be merged into the main branch (used heavily in team settings)

Want me to go deeper on any part — like branching, pull requests, or how to handle your first merge conflict?

Now i have done step 1 and 2.

Please explain the next steps more into depth

08.38

Let's walk through Steps 3 and 4 in detail. There are two ways to start Step 3 — which fits your situation?

Q: How do you want to start your first project? A: Create a new repo on GitHub first (recommended)

Q: What operating system are you on? A: Windows

08.39

Perfect. Here's your exact step-by-step guide for Windows.

Step 3: Create a repo on GitHub

  1. Go to github.com and click the + icon (top right) → New repository
  2. Give it a name (e.g. my-first-project), no spaces
  3. Check "Add a README file"
  4. Click Create repository

You'll land on your new repo's page. Now copy the URL — it looks like: https://github.com/yourname/my-first-project.git

Step 3b: Clone it to your computer

Open Git Bash (search for it in your Start menu — it was installed with Git).

Navigate to where you want your project to live, then clone it:

bash
cd Desktop          # or wherever you want the folder
git clone https://github.com/yourname/my-first-project.git
cd my-first-project # enter the folder

You now have a local copy of your repo on your computer, fully connected to GitHub.

Step 4: The daily loop in practice

Say you create a file called hello.txt inside the folder. Here's what you do:

bash
# 1. Check what Git sees (always safe to run)
git status

# 2. Stage your changes
git add .

# 3. Commit with a message describing what you did
git commit -m "Add hello.txt"

# 4. Push to GitHub
git push

After git push, refresh your GitHub repo page in the browser — your file will be there.

What git status looks like at each stage:

V

visualize

V

visualize show_widget

A few tips for Windows specifically:

  • Always use Git Bash, not Command Prompt or PowerShell — it understands all Git commands properly
  • The first time you git push, Windows may pop up a browser window asking you to log in to GitHub — just follow it through, and it'll remember you after that
  • If you ever get stuck, git status is always safe to run and tells you exactly what state you're in

Try it now: Create any file inside your cloned folder (even a simple .txt), then run through the 4 commands. Once you see your file appear on GitHub in the browser, you've got the full loop down.

Want to learn about branches next, or do you want to try this first and come back with any questions?

This is what it does in step 3

08.43

Haha, no worries — a couple of small mistakes here, easy to fix!

Problem 1: cd Desktop failed because Git Bash started you in C:/Users/norup and your Desktop path might be slightly different. Try this instead:

bash
cd ~/Desktop

The ~ is a shortcut for your home folder and works reliably on Windows.

Problem 2: You typed what does that mean directly into the terminal — the terminal only understands commands, not plain English! Only type the commands I give you.

Problem 3: You typed clone ... but the command is git clone. Every Git command starts with the word git.

So here's exactly what to type, line by line:

bash
cd ~/Desktop

(press Enter, then type the next line)

bash
git clone https://github.com/magnusnorup/firstproject.git

(press Enter)

bash
cd firstproject

Make sure you copy the URL directly from your GitHub repo page — click the green Code button on your repo and copy the HTTPS link from there.

Give that a go and paste a screenshot of what you get!

Great news — the clone worked perfectly! 🎉

The cd ~/Desktop error doesn't matter at all — Git Bash just cloned the folder into C:/Users/norup instead, which is totally fine. Your project is there.

Now type these two lines:

bash
cd firstproject
git status

You should see something like "nothing to commit, working tree clean" — that means you're inside your repo and everything is connected to GitHub. Send a screenshot and we'll move to the next step!

08.46

That's perfect! Everything is working exactly as it should. Notice a couple of things:

  • The prompt now shows ~/firstproject (main) — you're inside your repo, on the main branch
  • "nothing to commit, working tree clean" — Git is watching the folder and everything is in sync with GitHub

Now let's make your first real commit. Create a file inside the folder:

bash
echo "Hello GitHub!" > hello.txt
git status

You should now see hello.txt listed in red as an untracked file. Then run the full loop:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Add hello.txt"
git push

After that, go to github.com/magnusnorup/firstproject in your browser and refresh — you'll see hello.txt sitting there live on GitHub.

Almost there! Git just needs to know who you are before it can save commits. It's asking you to set your name and email — run these two commands with your own details:

bash
git config --global user.email "your@email.com"
git config --global user.name "Magnus"

Use the same email you signed up to GitHub with. Then just commit and push again:

bash
git commit -m "Add hello.txt"
git push

git config --global user.name "Magnus"

git push

08.52

You did it!! Your first ever commit is live on GitHub! 🎉

You can see it worked:

  • "1 file changed, 1 insertion" — Git saved your hello.txt
  • "main -> main" — it pushed successfully to GitHub

Go to github.com/magnusnorup/firstproject in your browser right now and you'll see hello.txt sitting there!

There's one small note — GitHub is telling you your repo URL changed from magnusnorup to Magnusnorup (capital M). Run this once to update it:

bash
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/Magnusnorup/firstproject.git

That just keeps everything tidy going forward.

You've now completed the full loop:

  • ✅ Created a repo on GitHub
  • ✅ Cloned it to your computer
  • ✅ Made a file
  • ✅ Staged, committed and pushed it

The hardest part is done — it gets very natural very quickly from here. Want to learn about branches next, which is where GitHub gets really powerful?

YEs please

08.54

Branches are one of the most powerful things about Git. Here's the idea:

Imagine your project is a timeline. A branch lets you step off that timeline, experiment or build something new, and then merge it back in when it's ready — without ever touching the main working code.

This is how every professional developer works.

V

visualize

V

visualize show_widget

The commands are simple. Let's do it for real right now in your terminal:

Create and switch to a new branch:

bash
git checkout -b my-new-feature

Your prompt will change from (main) to (my-new-feature) — you're now on a separate branch.

Make a change on it:

bash
echo "This is my feature" > feature.txt
git add .
git commit -m "Add feature.txt"

Push the branch to GitHub:

bash
git push -u origin my-new-feature

Then merge it back into main:

bash
git checkout main
git merge my-new-feature
git push

The key thing to understand: while you're on my-new-feature, the main branch is completely untouched. If you switch back to main mid-way, feature.txt won't even be visible — it only exists on the branch. That's what makes branches so powerful for experimenting safely.

Give it a go and screenshot what you get!

Here is what i get :)

08.55

That is absolutely flawless! Everything worked perfectly! 🎉

Here's what happened step by step:

  • ✅ Created my-new-feature branch and pushed it to GitHub
  • ✅ Switched back to main with git checkout main
  • ✅ Merged the feature in — "Fast-forward, 1 file changed"
  • ✅ Pushed to GitHub — "main -> main"

Go check your GitHub repo in the browser — you'll now see both hello.txt AND feature.txt sitting on main.

You've now learned the complete professional Git workflow:

CommandWhat it does
git cloneDownload a repo to your computer
git statusSee what's changed
git add .Stage all changes
git commit -m "..."Save a snapshot
git pushSend to GitHub
git pullGet latest from GitHub
git checkout -b nameCreate a new branch
git merge nameMerge a branch into current

This is genuinely how developers at every level — from beginners to senior engineers — work every single day. You've got the fundamentals down solid.

Want to learn about pull requests next? That's how teams review each other's code on GitHub before merging — and it's the thing you'll see most in any real job or open source project.

The things is just done, can you explain more about what is essential does?

08.58

Great question. Let me break down what each thing you just did actually means under the hood — not just what to type, but what Git is really doing.

git init / git clone When you cloned the repo, Git created a hidden folder called .git inside your project. That folder is Git — it contains the entire history of every change ever made to the project. Your actual files are just the surface; the real magic lives in .git.

git add . This doesn't save anything yet. It just tells Git "these are the changes I want to include in my next save." You can think of it like putting items in a shopping basket before you check out. The . means "everything changed" — you can also add individual files like git add hello.txt.

git commit -m "..." This is the actual save. Git takes a permanent snapshot of everything you staged and stores it with a unique ID (like 65bae11), your name, your email, a timestamp, and your message. You can always go back to any commit in history — nothing is ever truly deleted.

git push Your commits up to this point only exist on your computer. Push uploads them to GitHub so they're backed up in the cloud and visible to anyone with access to the repo.

git pull The opposite of push. If someone else (or you on another computer) made changes on GitHub, pull downloads them to your local copy. Always git pull before you start working to make sure you're up to date.

Branches This is the big one. Here's the real mental model:

V

visualize

V

visualize show_widget

A branch isn't a copy of your project — it's just a label pointing to a commit. When you create a new branch, Git doesn't duplicate any files. It just creates a new pointer. That's why branches are instant and lightweight.

git merge When you merged my-new-feature into main, Git looked at where the two branches diverged, figured out what changed on each, and combined them. The message "Fast-forward" you saw means main hadn't moved since you branched off — so Git just slid the main label forward to the latest commit. No conflict, no fuss.

The commit ID (like 65bae11) Every commit gets a unique fingerprint generated from its content. This means if anyone tampers with history, the IDs change — Git history is essentially tamper-proof. You can use these IDs to jump back to any point in history with git checkout 65bae11.

The big picture is this: Git is essentially a time machine for your code. Every commit is a restore point. Branches let you explore without risk. Push and pull let you sync that time machine across computers and teammates.

Want to learn about pull requests next — which is how this all works in a team setting?

What if i wanted to publish a website with data i got from you or lovable - Would github also be the thing for that?

08.59

Yes! GitHub has a free feature called GitHub Pages that lets you host a website directly from your repo — no server, no cost, no extra setup needed.

But the answer depends a bit on what you're publishing:

Q: What kind of site are you thinking of publishing? A: I'm not sure yet

08.59

No problem! Here's how it all fits together so you can decide when the time comes:

V

visualize

V

visualize show_widget

The key insight is that GitHub is always involved regardless — it stores your code, and the hosting platform watches your repo and publishes it automatically every time you push.

Here's what each path looks like in practice:

Simple HTML/CSS site → GitHub Pages Completely free, built right into GitHub. You just enable it in your repo settings and your site is live at magnusnorup.github.io/firstproject. Perfect for portfolios, landing pages, or anything Claude helped you write in plain HTML.

React or Lovable app → Vercel or Netlify Lovable actually has its own one-click publishing built in. But if you export the code, Vercel is the go-to — you connect it to your GitHub repo and every git push automatically deploys your site. Also free for personal projects.

Site with a backend or database → Railway or Render If your site needs to store data, handle logins, or run server code, you need a proper server. These platforms handle that, but they cost a small amount per month once you go beyond hobby use.

Since you're just starting out, GitHub Pages is the perfect first step — want me to walk you through publishing your firstproject repo as a live website right now? It takes about 2 minutes.

Yes lets try to publish a website

09.00

Let's make a proper little website to publish. First, let's create an HTML file in your repo.

In Git Bash, make sure you're in your project folder (you should see ~/firstproject (main)) and run this:

bash
echo "" > index.html

This creates the file. Now we need to put real HTML in it — but instead of typing it all in the terminal, let's use a proper text editor.

Open the folder in File Explorer:

bash
explorer .

That opens your firstproject folder. You'll see index.html sitting there.

Right-click index.html → Open with → Notepad (or VS Code if you have it).

Then paste this in and save:

html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Magnus's First Site</title>
    <style>
      body {
        font-family: sans-serif;
        max-width: 600px;
        margin: 80px auto;
        text-align: center;
        background: #f9f9f9;
        color: #333;
      }
      h1 { color: #4f46e5; }
      p { font-size: 18px; line-height: 1.6; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <h1>Hey, I'm Magnus 👋</h1>
    <p>This is my first website, published with GitHub Pages.</p>
    <p>Built with help from Claude.</p>
  </body>
</html>

Save the file, then come back to Git Bash and push it:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Add index.html"
git push

Screenshot when done — then I'll show you how to turn it live with GitHub Pages in about 30 seconds!

Run through the steps more in more depth please

09.04

Of course! Let's go step by step.

Step 1: Make sure you're in the right folder

Open Git Bash and type:

bash
cd ~/firstproject

Your prompt should show ~/firstproject (main). Screenshot that so we know we're starting in the right place!

Step 2: Create the HTML file

Type this exactly:

bash
echo "" > index.html

This creates an empty file called index.html in your project folder.

Step 3: Open the folder in File Explorer

Type:

bash
explorer .

Don't forget the dot — it means "open this folder". A File Explorer window will pop up and you'll see your files including index.html.

Step 4: Open index.html in Notepad

In the File Explorer window:

  1. Right-click on index.html
  2. Click Open with
  3. Click Notepad

A blank Notepad window opens.

Step 5: Paste the website code

Copy everything below and paste it into Notepad:

html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Magnus's First Site</title>
    <style>
      body {
        font-family: sans-serif;
        max-width: 600px;
        margin: 80px auto;
        text-align: center;
        background: #f9f9f9;
        color: #333;
      }
      h1 { color: #4f46e5; }
      p { font-size: 18px; line-height: 1.6; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <h1>Hey, I'm Magnus 👋</h1>
    <p>This is my first website, published with GitHub Pages.</p>
    <p>Built with help from Claude.</p>
  </body>
</html>

Then press Ctrl + S to save.

Step 6: Push it to GitHub

Go back to Git Bash and run these one at a time:

bash
git add .
bash
git commit -m "Add index.html"
bash
git push

Take it one step at a time and send a screenshot after each one — starting with Step 1!

html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Magnus's First Site</title>
    <style>
      body {
        font-family: sans-serif;
        max-width: 600px;
        margin: 80px auto;
        text-align: center;
        background: #f9f9f9;
        color: #333;

    <p>This is my first website, published with GitHub Pages.</p>
    <p>Built with help from Claude.</p>
  </body>
</html>

Step 3: Save it in the right place Press Ctrl + S and a save dialog opens. Now this is important:

  1. In the top bar navigate to C:/Users/norup/firstproject
  2. Change the filename from *.txt to index.html
  3. Change "Save as type" to "All files (.)" — this is critical, otherwise it saves as index.html.txt
  4. Click Save

09.13

YOU HAVE A LIVE WEBSITE! 🎉

That URL — https://magnusnorup.github.io/firstproject — is public. Anyone in the world can visit it right now. Send it to a friend!

And here's the really powerful part: every time you want to update your website from now on, you just edit index.html in Notepad, then run these three commands:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "describe your change"
git push

And your live website updates automatically within about 60 seconds. No uploading files, no FTP, no complicated hosting — just push and it's live.

Think about what you did today from scratch:

  • ✅ Installed Git
  • ✅ Created a GitHub account
  • ✅ Cloned a repo to your computer
  • ✅ Learned add, commit, push
  • ✅ Created and merged a branch
  • ✅ Published a live website to the internet

That's a genuinely solid foundation — most people take weeks to get this far. Want to spruce up the website itself next, or is there another direction you want to take this?

But would VS code be stronger to run it through than notepad?

09.14

Yes, absolutely — VS Code is in a completely different league to Notepad. It's what virtually every professional developer uses daily.

Here's why it's so much better:

Notepad is just a basic text editor — no awareness of code at all. It's like writing with a pencil.

VS Code is built specifically for code and gives you things Notepad never could:

  • Color coding — different parts of your code light up in different colors so it's instantly readable
  • Auto-complete — it suggests what you're about to type as you go
  • Error highlighting — it underlines mistakes before you even run anything
  • Built-in terminal — you can run your Git commands right inside VS Code, no need to switch between windows
  • File sidebar — see your entire project folder at a glance

The built-in terminal is the biggest win for you right now — instead of juggling Git Bash and Notepad separately, everything lives in one place.

Download it free at code.visualstudio.com — it's made by Microsoft, works great on Windows.

Once installed, you can open your whole project in it by going to Git Bash and typing:

bash
code .

That opens VS Code with your entire firstproject folder loaded and ready. Want me to walk you through setting it up?

Try it right now — type git status in the terminal and hit Enter. You should see "nothing to commit, working tree clean" confirming everything is in sync with GitHub!

it did this

09.28

The terminal in VS Code is using PowerShell which doesn't know Git. We need to switch it to Git Bash instead.

Click the dropdown arrow next to "powershell" in the terminal bar, then click Select Default Profile, then choose Git Bash from the list that appears at the top of the screen.

After that, close the terminal with the bin icon and open a new one with Terminal → New Terminal. It should now show the Git Bash prompt and work perfectly!

Now done which bin icon should i press?

09.30

I can see the dropdown is showing — click bash in that dropdown right now. That will switch the terminal to Git Bash instantly — no need to close anything!

Perfect i think we are running now

09.31

Amazing! Try typing git status in the terminal now — you should see the familiar Git Bash prompt and it should work perfectly this time. Screenshot what you get!

09.31

That is perfect! You now have the complete professional setup:

  • ✅ Code with color highlighting at the top
  • ✅ Git Bash terminal at the bottom showing nothing to commit, working tree clean
  • ✅ Branch indicator showing (main)
  • ✅ Everything in one window — no more switching between apps

From now on your entire workflow is:

  1. Edit index.html in the top panel
  2. Save with Ctrl + S
  3. Run git add . → git commit -m "..." → git push in the bottom terminal
  4. Your live website at magnusnorup.github.io/firstproject updates in ~60 seconds

Want to try it right now? Click on line 19 in your code where it says Hey, I'm Magnus and change the text to something else, save it, then push it and watch your live website update!

done

09.32

Amazing! Now run these in the terminal at the bottom:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Update homepage text"
git push

Then wait about 60 seconds and refresh https://magnusnorup.github.io/firstproject in your browser — you should see your change live on the internet!

My sites doesnt update with the new text

09.34

No worries, it sometimes takes a minute or two. A couple of things to check:

1. Make sure the push actually worked — run this in the terminal:

bash
git status

It should say "nothing to commit, working tree clean". Screenshot what you see!

2. When you refresh the site — make sure you do a hard refresh to clear the browser cache:

  • Press Ctrl + Shift + R instead of just F5

If the push worked but the site still hasn't updated after 2-3 minutes, we can check the GitHub Pages build status together!

Still doenst show up

09.36

Let's check what's happening. Run this in the terminal:

bash
git status

And screenshot it — I want to make sure the changes actually got pushed to GitHub first!

09.36

I can see two things:

1. The push says "Everything up-to-date" — which means your latest edit hasn't been saved and pushed yet. Look at the tab at the top — it shows a dot next to index.html which means you have unsaved changes!

2. Press Ctrl + S to save first, then run:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Update homepage text"
git push

The dot on the tab disappears when it's saved. Always save before pushing!

How about now - Still not updated

09.38

Almost! I can see two issues:

  1. You typed git push~ instead of git push — there's a tilde (~) at the end which made it fail
  2. The file still shows M (modified) next to it — meaning it's not committed yet

Run these one at a time:

bash
git add .
git commit -m "Update homepage text"
git push

Then do a hard refresh on your site with Ctrl + Shift + R!

Now it works!

09.40

Excellent! 🎉 You now have the full professional workflow down:

  1. Edit code in VS Code
  2. Save with Ctrl + S
  3. Push with git add . → git commit -m "..." → git push
  4. Hard refresh with Ctrl + Shift + R to see changes live

A key lesson from today — always check for the dot on the tab in VS Code, it means unsaved changes. And always make sure the push says "main -> main" not "Everything up-to-date", which means nothing new was sent.

You've come a really long way today Magnus — from never having used Git to having a live website you can update from VS Code in seconds. That's genuinely impressive for one session.

Where do you want to take it from here? A few directions you could go:

  • Make the website look better — I can help you build a proper personal site or portfolio with HTML/CSS
  • Learn more Git — pull requests, handling merge conflicts, collaborating with others
  • Connect it to Lovable — build something more powerful and deploy it properly

What sounds most interesting?

Yes, let make a proper site - I would like it to have focus on helping people with answering sales emails the right way with help of AI.

Can you do that?

09.43

Love the idea! Before I build it, let me get a feel for what you want:

Q: What style are you going for? A: Clean & professional (white, minimal)

Q: What sections should the site have? (Select all that apply) A: Hero / headline, How it works, Contact / waitlist signup, Pricing

Q: Is this a real product you're building or a concept for now? A: Just a concept for now

09.43

Perfect — let me build that for you now!

Viewed a file, created a file, read a file

Here's your site! It includes all four sections — hero with a live email preview, how it works, pricing, and a waitlist signup.

Now to get it live, just replace the content of your index.html in VS Code with this new code:

  1. Click on index.html in VS Code
  2. Press Ctrl + A to select everything
  3. Delete it and paste in the new code from the downloaded file
  4. Press Ctrl + S to save
  5. Then in the terminal:
bash
git add .
git commit -m "Launch ReplyRight landing page"
git push

Wait 60 seconds, hard refresh with Ctrl + Shift + R and your site is live! Want any changes to the design, copy, or sections?
